title: Reggie E -Technical Discussion Interview | Data Scientist
meeting_title: Reggie E -Technical Discussion Interview | Data Scientist

transcript.text: This meeting is being recorded.
Hello.
How are you?
Hey, doing well.
Can you hear me okay?
Yeah, I can hear you well.
Okay, great.
How are you doing?
Pretty good.
Very nice to meet you.
Nice to meet you as well.
Happy to chat today.
How would you like me to pronounce your name?
Is it Reggie?
Yeah, Reggie's fine.
That's what I heard from Daniel, so that's perfect.
I'm Katja.
Again, very nice to meet you.
So I am an advisor to your growthx, and Daniel asked me to be part of this interview process for data roles.
So what I would like to do today with you...

I can share a little bit more about myself, but I would love also to hear about you, your recent work, and would love to deep dive in one of your projects, and then we will have a little case study on the growthx, if that sounds fun to you.
Yeah, that sounds great.
Yeah, I can go ahead and share about me and my career and my work.
Absolutely.
So I've held various roles across data science and machine learning.
I've been on the more software engineering ML ops side at times, and I've done the experimentation, analytics, forecasting piece as well.
You I try to maintain a good mix of both in my career, which is actually fairly hard to do, because I feel like you usually get pushed hard in one direction or the other.
That's true.
Yes, that's true.
true.
I…

I primarily consider myself a data scientist, meaning I love working with data about real impactful decisions, business decisions, and using appropriate tools, whether that's simple dashboard or whether it's a traditional machine learning model or whether it's a deep learning model, applied AI using LLMs and vision models, et cetera.
So my current role is at a company called Integral Ad Science.
So IAS, it sits between users of platforms like YouTube, TikTok, Snapchat, and all social media platforms, the platforms themselves, and advertisers who want to advertise on those platforms.
So the basic idea is if you're an advertiser, say like Disney, you want to show your ad on YouTube, you know, there's certain things you don't want to show your ad next to, any sort of.
Violent content, profanity, drugs, alcohol, anything like that.
But there's also certain categories you may want to show your ad next to.
So like if you're Tesla, you want to show your ad on conversations about electric vehicles or something like that.

So to do this at scale, because IAS processes like a huge chunk of multimodal data, like every hour across social media, I build and my team, we build models to detect content, what's in content at scale.
So using LLMs on text and metadata, vision models on images and video, audio models, I've been involved a lot with our speech-to-text or automatic speech recognition pipeline.
So this work involves looking at off the...
Jet capabilities, know, latest open weights models that come from different providers, seeing if we need to fine tune those, if we need to train our own models from some pre-trained backbone.

So a lot of evaluating the capabilities out there and then where necessary, like doing that, either fine tuning their training piece, pushing those to production, and then evaluating how they're doing in production.

And if we say like, oh, we have a new model to do something, running a small scale experiment on a slice of the social media fire hose, and trying to estimate the performance of the new model and what it would look like if we roll it out at scale.
And the last piece is making sure we don't amass a huge AWS budget when we do all that.
So worrying about inference costs and inference efficiency, as well as latency, although...
A lot of the stuff we do is offline in the sense that, like, it doesn't have to be super, super fast.
It just can't be super slow.
So it's really about the offline training and inference costs from the efficiency standpoint.
So, yeah, I can just show people.
more why, what is the purpose of detecting the content in the first place?
All right.
So we essentially work for the platform.
So, like, we will work for YouTube or we work for Facebook, Meta.
And they have their own content filtering specialists, human annotators.
But before that level, there's a lot of automation that goes on.
So they have their own teams, but they actually outsource a lot of that to IAS or one of our competitors.
So it's basically the first step in filtering appropriate or inappropriate content.
And some of what they do is just to like avoid legal risk or whatever.
Mostly it's for advertising, right?
These platforms make money off advertising.

So if they're trying to sell an ad bundle to a big company, that company wants to make sure it's what's known as brand safe or brand aligned, which means like, yeah, they won't, they don't, there's certain things they just don't want to advertise next to.
That makes a lot of sense.
This is so interesting.
How big is the company and how big is the team actually?
So it's funny.
I work fully remote.
It's a hard question to answer.
I work remote too, so I feel you.
My team, there are six of us reporting to my manager.
And we sit in a bigger data science engineering team that's about 50 people.
And there are five other people.
We data science teams that range from 10 to 50 people, data science and engineering.
And then we have our back-end engineers who just make sure the whole ship keeps running, connect with AWS and other servers and our CICD pipelines.
So that's maybe another 20 folks.
So engineering as a whole is probably worldwide 300.
But then I think there's like a thousand or more people in sales and marketing and product and business development who I never interact with ever.
And it's a global company.
So I think most people are not based in the U.S.
So it's like Dublin, Italy, Paris, also Japan and or Korea, South America as
So, yeah, I think if you got everybody in a room, it'd be a lot bigger than I can estimate.
That sounds fun.

Also, a lot of people in terms of, so you're saying your team is six, but the bigger data science team, could you share more about like, what's the separation between the teams and what is like your team is specifically focusing on?
So, my team specifically focuses on what I was talking about, the content understanding piece.
So, we, you know, we basically talk directly to our CTO and our head of product about what kinds of algorithms that they want to sell to customers.
And we talk about what's possible with data science and machine learning.
We, like, set the roadmap, and so, like, what usually happens at the beginning of a quarter, I'll say, like, oh, I'm going to take on this model.
Model to do whatever or this task.
Here's how we could accomplish it over maybe one quarter, two quarter.
Like I set that out.
My manager goes, okay, you commit to this and then get the data needs, train or do whatever I have to do on the modeling aspect.
Get that into deployment with some of our backend engineers, but, you know, hands on on the deployment and monitoring aspect as well.
So, and then the other data science teams do like, so like there's a team that works on advertising fraud, for example, you know.
So there's like basically a similar, but like by themes, right?
By areas.
Uh-huh.
That's so interesting.
So basically what I understand what you're doing is like really fine-tuning, developing, evaluating models, right?
And then also hands-on on deployment, but there's also a supportive engineering team who helps with deployment.
That's right.
Yeah.
And so some of this is like...
Not standardized.
So let me give you an example.
I was working on our speech-to-text automatic speech recognition pipeline.
And that I essentially like evaluated some open weight models.
Okay.
And then I, most of my work was on the deployment side, because that piece of our stack was like a lot of legacy code and like really messy.
So that was like 90% deployment work, 10% modeling ML work.
But in some other things I've had where our deployment infrastructure is more like mature, it'll be like 90% data science ML work.
And then deployment piece is like, just upload some weights, a Docker container, and then like, that's all good to go.
That sounds fantastic.
And in terms of deploying, because your company is working for like YouTube, et cetera, for the clients, how do you deploy everything on your infrastructure and then let them use?
The API?
Essentially, yeah.
Ah.
Okay, that makes sense.
And then you mentioned also part of the role as well as experimentation and A-B testing.
Could you walk me into that part?
Right.
So it's the experiments we do are a little different than the typical, you know, A-B test the blue button versus a green button.
But when we have a model, we do a lot of evaluation in-house on, you know, precision, recall, iPhone, whatever.
But then when we roll it out to production, obviously, you never know how it's going to perform.
And so we have to find markets and specify segments and experimentation, like, lengths to assess the true performance.
So it's not a feature, it's a model.
So if we have a model.
model, if I train a model, we think of the one I actually did.
So I had a model to detect celebrities' faces.
cases and images, right?
So this set of a bunch of different use cases.
So it's like, okay, we have a new celebrity detection pipeline.
We have tons of pipelines, call them.
I would roll it out to maybe one social media platform.
So US TikTok users, I believe this was.
And so over a period of time, and it actually doesn't take long given the amount of data we process at once.
So over like a week, you can see how does the model performance compare.
One of the hiccups is usually, if you totally own the product, you can look at false positives and false negatives.
We only see, in most cases, false positives.
So if I put a pipeline to detect celebrity faces, and you're on some platform where it's like, you can't do celebrity faces.
And the model, like Facebook flags your account, says you uploaded a picture with a celebrity in it.
You're like, no.
That's not a celebrity.
That's just me.
That's how I look.
You can dispute that with a social media platform, and that's called a flag.
So what we track are flag rates.
We have SLAs with social media platforms about flag rates, and essentially false positive rates.
So internally, when I'm building a model and evaluating it, I can see false positives, false negatives, and thus precision recall F1.
But when we deploy it, we see false positives when we get flags.
So that's already a little noisy.
We do not observe false negatives.
Like, not directly.
We observe false negatives when it's been like two or three quarters, and our model sucks.
And then somebody at Facebook emails somebody on our product team, and it's like, your product sucks, because we're missing a lot.
So we don't get false negatives.
So it, like, presents some interesting estimation challenges.
And he's like, when I was at Netflix,
Netflix is the more traditional, we're rolling this out to a user base, fix, you know, sample period, effect sizes, et cetera, et cetera, RC, randomized control tile type, trial type business.
But here's a little different.
Makes a lot of sense.
But the principles are essentially similar, especially like this is the observation more.
And in terms of like A-B test, if you have any specific tasks there, it will be very similar as well, right?
Do you mean like by A-B testing is comparing also the models, like older version with the new version?
So we'll run in shadow mode or in deployment, the current model and the potentially new model with certain things like the speech-to-text project.
That one, like the internal estimation of quality is like really good.
But what we want to see is when we deploy.
The new cheaper version, because it was about decreasing our inference costs for speech-to-text, which is, like, very expensive.
So I was like, okay, we know this will be cheaper, so we'll just roll it out as broadly as possible.
And we're not as much worried about flag rates or anything like that.
The only thing is, like, so let's say you have a speech-to-text model in production.
It captures audio from video, and then it transcribes it into text.
And you put a new one into production.
Keep the old one for, like, a week while the new one is running side-by-side on, like, representative sample.
So, like, well-matched samples.
And that's, like, tricky when you're dealing with audio.
It needs to be the same language.
It needs to be across the same platforms.
And then you measure the performance in terms of transcription quality for those two models kind of running side-by-side.
Does that make sense?
Yeah, don't even make sense.
And it sounds like also you're working with a lot of like very recent tech, right?
So it's LLMs, VLMs.
What was your most, maybe the favorite project you've done and the one you're most proud of?
Oh, let's see.
The celebrity one was cool actually because it wasn't using the very recent techs.
That was like classic CV bounding box type stuff, which I did very How do you do the techs?
Like it's because we have like so many celebrities.
Do you have like a labeled data?
Yeah.
So that was the other thing.
We have a taxonomy team that keeps, so there was two pieces.
Like actually I think this is one of the first tasks like I got assigned.
So we have a database of just pictures.
database of
Celebrities.

Sometimes there's a new celebrity or just like a notable figure, like maybe somebody who's running for office in a certain election and there's celebrity, but like now they're in the news and they're on social media a lot more.
So there the task is to maintain a running count of similar faces.
So like if I see, you know, a new policy, I see a face and then like you calculate features from that feature vectors using some pre-trained vision model, you hash it.
And if I see that again in a certain time period, and then again in a certain time period, it accumulates.
So we like now this is a celebrity, like this is a new celebrity because they're showing up more.
And so it's like either somebody on the taxonomy team will like flag it for us, which makes it easy.
But sometimes things happen in real time, you know, especially when there are recent.
Tragic events in the U.S., and then, like, somebody notable is in the news.
Like, if you really want to be on that, then you can't always wait for a domain specialist to integrate that into our, like, database of notable figures.

But that project wasn't my favorite, but it was interesting because, yeah, it used some, like, VLMs, but it was a lot of, like, classic computer vision geometry type stuff, like figuring out, how to get good bounding boxes on these images, to center things, how to deal with noise and variance and image quality and things like that.
My actual favorite one was also in the computer vision space a little bit.
No, yeah, I guess a lot of it.
So this is when I was at Netflix, and I worked on a very similar, in a very similar problem space.
So Netflix has tons of movies and TV shows.
And if you were to go to Netflix and you go to some TV show, an episode like Stranger Things, you'll have these tags that say like suspense, horror, like whatever.
You may see two or three.
I don't think you'll ever see more than four.
But internally at Netflix, there's like 55,000 of these tags.
And Netflix also has a taxonomy team that writes long documents about what this means, what does horror mean, what does suspense mean, what does comedic versus humorous mean.
And this is all done by hand by humans for like 15 years plus years.
So my role was to use AI, data science, machine learning to automate a good chunk of this process.
So this would let you expand coverage.
So Netflix generates this metadata, these tags for like the most popular titles, but there are like some Scandinavian or like Brazilian titles that may.
be only a few thousand people watch, or they actually don't even do it.
And so it's like, okay, can we automate this process somewhat, or at least human in the loop, and expand the coverage?
So that's at a high level.
At a low level, it involves feeding in all the different sources of information about, let's say, a movie.
So you give it the script, you give it the dialogue, so that's text.
Then you have the audio waveforms, the speech, and music.
Those are two different things, the speech versus the music.
Then you also have the video itself.
you know, video, we downsample it into images.
Once you have images, you can use any, like, image model, computer vision, vision transformers, whatever.
And so it was, like, what I was doing most of my time there was trying to extract information at a scene level from all of these different information sources.
So I got to use early LLMs,
I started on this before ChatGPT was launched, and when ChatGPT was launched, it just became like, no, this is what we're doing now.
So jumping on that train really early in late 2022, using off-the-shelf vision models, so vision encoders.
like, okay, now we had this computer vision pipeline.
Somebody had built some models for representing, but like, oh, we can use all the stuff that's coming up on Hugging Face or whatever and use that.

So getting a lot of experience trying to understand content at scale, but then also seeing the impact of like this new, these new metadata tags and impacts, like my teams, our taxonomy teams within Netflix.
So, you know, they spend a lot of time, like, they'll just like watch a scene, three seconds, pause it, write stuff down.
Plus press play, watch it for three or four seconds, pause it, write stuff down, which is just crazy.
Like, like, like building a tool.
Automate that process for them.
like, okay, you can still do this.
But given the model I built, it says it's probably this in the scene.
So where it may have taken you, you know, three seconds to watch, plus like, five seconds to type in your notes per scene.
Now it's maybe like, just two or three seconds, because you have the suggestions, you go through it once, and you can just hit accept or reject.

So like, seeing the internal adoption on that kind of tool, but then later saying, oh, what if we roll this out on the platform in certain areas, and then seeing user adoption for this kind of output as well.
That makes a lot of sense.
And there's a lot of stuff that you would do, probably in your role right now to the client.
And Netflix was also more like internal improvements, right?
Is there any, maybe within this?

projects, but maybe also any other project where you saw a huge business impact to your work, and basically that you would, if you have any example that you initiated the conversation, how to find the solution, and then make this model that, or evaluation idea projects you did that had a huge business impact.
Yeah, so I'll give two.
So one is part of that project at Netflix.
So we had the internal use case, but also the user-facing, product-facing one, which is for what we call lower-tier titles, those less-watched titles, expanding the coverage.
So I believe there's a sample of titles in, like, among the, like, Norwegian countries, where, you know, I work with our globalization and localization team, who helped.

Like, you know, people across the world discover stuff, like using this new metadata on a bunch of titles in, like, these European countries that, like, did not have good coverage and seeing, like, okay, can we take ones which are, like, little to no coverage?
I mean, there's some there.
There's always some description, but the description is just kind of, like, largely placeholders for these lower tier titles.
All right, bring that out to this sample, this user base, and seeing if it impacts.
So the goal here was not to reduce the number of watches because it's like we don't have anything there yet.
So we put anything there, and it increases, great.
But as long as it doesn't decrease, the metric we use is qualified play days.
That doesn't mean a whole lot.
But as long as it doesn't reduce our watch time metric, then, like, we go for...
And so that was a really big impact as far as increasing the scope of our title coverage.
So we used the term discoverability at Netflix.
increased the ability of people to discover.
Usually when you run an AP test, it's very clean.
It's like, okay, we increase watches by X percent or we decrease costs.
This was going from like almost no coverage, meaning the title, the metadata, the tags, or the descriptions were like basically non-existent, like full coverage, which is a little harder to quantify.
I mean, like, you know, technically it's like infinite percent.
It's not infinite business dollars, but it did unlock, you know, a whole new region where we could expand the metadata for coverability.
And like, oh, if you're a user in these countries and these other markets, you actually have much more insight into the titles you could be interested in.
That long.
Long-term impacts like retention and churn rates and things like that.
So it's like, you know, a little harder to quantify than the typical sort of A-B test or experiment we run.
I think one that's easier to quantify is a project I actually had at Redfin, which was prior to Netflix, Redfin, the real estate company.
And so the goal was, so if you go to the Redfin website, you can enter in a home, your home, anybody else's home, and see an estimate of the home's value.
And so the idea was, if instead of just saying, if I sold my home, I bought this home right now, what would the price be?
We could build a model to show you over the next 12, technically 13 months, what would the price of that look like?
You know, it's worth maybe.
If you sold it a year from now, it's worth less.
If you sold it six months from now, it's worth more within some error bars or whatever.
So my team, we were the economics team.
So I was tasked with actually building this model and working with our product team to how to display it to both users on redfin.com or the app and the real estate agents.
So Redfin has a bunch of real estate agents on the ground, and they have an app that looks different from what you as a consumer see, but they also have their own app.
And so the idea was give this information to people who visit the app or the website, also give this information to real estate agents when they're showing houses.

And can we see some either increase in site visits from displaying this, but then also the other metric for real estate agents, we use net promoter score, which is like, okay, if we show this to real estate agents, does it improve?
By their own perception, does it improve their experience using the product?
As far as site visits, we rolled this out and actually saw a really big boost in site visits.
I can't remember the numbers.
It was at least like 5% to 10% over the period we ran it.
And then for real estate agents, it's just like overwhelmingly positive net promoter score.
So unfortunately, what you're describing is like forecasting the house value or because you also mentioned forecasting a value in the future.
So it's not only like current estimate.
So were you working on both of those features or just the forecasting in the future?
The forecasting in the future.
So there's a team that owned or owns the like immediate forecast or the immediate home value called the retin estimate.
So our team.
I was tasked with taking that as a potential baseline.
I mean, you can obviously see some tension there because if the Redfin Now estimate is like X and we say it's going to be worth like 100X in a month, it's like nobody's going to trust that.
But like working with this team, using that Redfin Now estimate as a baseline and then trying to project it out like 12 months from that date.
So it's kind tricky.
How did you do that?
What was the solution?
So, I mean, it's a time series forecast, right?
So we have a massive history of home values in the U.S.
So, like, you know, if you go to Kaggle and see the home pricing challenge data set, it's like tiny.
But if you're actually a real estate agency, you have access to a massive database of home values going back.
I think it's.
Officially 40, no, much longer, longer than 40 years, even though, like, the data quality drops off a few decades past.
Then merging that in with macroeconomic data on, I mean, there's, like, you know, something like 2,000 macroeconomic series that are potentially relevant.

Everything from weekly jobs data to annual GDP, so, you know, it's a, it's a, one's a data engineering challenge, just getting that all aligned at the right sort of temporal consistency, monthly data versus weekly data, et cetera, but then also the time series forecasting, evaluating, thinking about which models make sense, thinking about how to evaluate them, really thinking about how to incorporate uncertainty in both the modeling process and in the product process and how we communicate the, the models and the forecasts to, to users and agents and even to executives.
So, you know, once you, it's not easy, but like once you have all the data aligned, it's just like a standard forecasting exercise.
So did you, did you use several models?
Is that what you're saying?
Yeah, so we had a massive ensemble.
Okay.
So one of my coworkers at the time was like, he was an economist and like very classically econometric minded.
So he produced a model that used just like a high dimensional vector autoaggression model, which is fine.
His was an input to my model because I was actually the product owner.
So one is just like for each.
So it's tricky because you have like, it's sort of a hierarchical nature.
So every unit, say like every zip code, every metro area is going to have some aggregate performance.
So like it increases.
There's 1% a year or like it has some time series of price change.
And then there's like one home within that zip code.
Part of its variation is going to be due to the zip code level.
And part of it is going to be just due to the specifics of that home, if that makes sense.

And so we built lots of different models at different hierarchies, at different levels of granularity, using different modeling types for simple auto-aggressive models, you know, exponential smoothing models.
I am personally a fan because I've done a lot of time series forecasts of using tabular machine learning models on time series data.
So I remember one of the things my manager at the time, who's also just like a classic econ guy, was really surprised by.
He's like, oh, if you're framing this as a tabular machine learning regression problem, how are you going to capture the temporal variation?
How
So if you're doing an autoregressive model or an exponential trend type model, it's just like, it's so fundamentally part of the, you know, it's an inductive bias in the model that time matters.
But in a, like an XGBoost model on lagged features, that's not necessarily the case.

So I actually had to show him that if you include the appropriate features, covariates, so like if, if home prices in the Bay Area move around over time, autoregressive model would just say they move around over time because they move around over time.
And they would just continue, and especially like a REMA model doesn't include any variables, right?
Like specifics for, for the house.
But if you say home prices in the Bay Area move around a lot because of weekly job reports in California, mean, or like, like what are the, why do home
When prices move around, like, it's not just because the earth moves around the sun, right?
There are causal factors here.
And if you include those features, a tabular machine learning model will capture the temporal variation.
And, like, I made this claim to him, which is, like, mathematically tight.
And I was like, oh, I actually have to go demonstrate this in the model and make sure it captures, like, the temporal variation, which it did.
That sounds fantastic.
So, at Redfin, and I'm looking at your resume as well, it looks like you were working a lot with the classical, traditional ML stuff.
How did you make the switch from Redfin to Netflix and working, like, specifically on the content and vision transformers?
Yeah, that's a good question.
The short answer is I don't know.
Um, the longer answer is when I
was hired into the role at Netflix.
They'd seen my background, which I had experience with classical NLP and computer vision models.
And just kind of the quirk of the way Netflix hires, it hires like very generally and then lets you choose a team.
And I was like really about this content understanding piece.
And so it started off using a lot of just the text data, classic NLP, not even using BERT or any.
Representation learning type encoders, just sort of like naive Bay style TF-IDF counts of frequencies and whatever.
And then once ChatGPT got announced, everybody doing everything at data science at Netflix had to pivot to using generative AI in their projects.
And so it was just a learning experience.
It's, you know, I feel like anybody who's worked in data science for the last five whatever years.
I Thank
I was fortunate, maybe unfortunate, to have that experience in sort of classic NLP and computer vision.
I think I was fortunate because I wasn't as hamstrung by my own biases about how to solve these problems.
I wasn't a machine learning researcher.
I recognized these things as tools, like using segmentation and boundy box detection and using Naive Bayes and TF-IDF and cosine similarity on these vectors.
Those are tools for understanding.
So I wasn't as fixated on those tools, but I had some domain knowledge about the quirks of dealing with text versus the quirks of dealing with images.

And, yeah, Netflix is a great place to come up to speed because we were all there were a lot of internal, like, sharing learning sessions where people were like, I did this in this project using this.
And then, like.
Oh, I can use this over here as well.
That's amazing.
Yeah.
And I also, my background is really a full stack data scientist, and I've been doing projects from, you know, NLP and computer vision.
But then I see that it's like sometimes it's difficult to switch because once in one area, it's like, okay, people expect you to, like, really go through this kind of path.
But for me, I switch more towards fintech and more like probably full stack generalist role.
Yeah.
Interesting.
The temptation to specialize is really, really high, right?
It's, like, I know it's some companies, larger companies.
It's,
You know, I was lucky to be at Netflix that allowed you and wanted you to be a generalist.
At other companies, I know like Facebook, no matter what, you're just like working on the narrowest of narrowest problems, which is fine as long as you stay at that company.
But if you try and branch out and go do something else, just like your skill set is very limited.
Exactly.
Well, what is your preference?
What do you like doing?
Broader or narrower?
Oh, for sure.
Broader.
Although it gets frustrating at times.
So this happened a lot at Netflix.
One of the frustrations I had there is that they love people being broad.
And there's like certain people in different areas who really go deep on topics.
But that's not really rewarded and it's very risky there.
But when you get to when you're tackling a problem and then you make a concrete improvement on it using whatever methods, then it's like move on and finding.
Another problem to, like, make a concrete improvement.
It's not like, oh, I did this as a first pass.
Like, this wasn't my project.
This was a project another team I worked on, which was a more time series forecasting problem, which I have a lot of experience in.
And they were like, oh, I did this using, like, it was only on Netflix server logs and using, looking at user activities.
Like, I did this simple, like, Arima thing and saw this improvement.
I'm in this presentation, like, oh, nice.
We can go really deep, and there's a lot of space to improve here.
And they were like, and my manager was like, yeah, yeah, but, like, this is good enough for now.
So let's keep finding the low-hanging fruit, like, regardless of what skills are required.

## Meeting Purpose

[Technical interview to assess Reggie Edwards's data science experience.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=40.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Broad Experience:** Reggie Edwards has a full-stack data science background, spanning ML engineering, MLOps, experimentation, and analytics.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=82.0)
  - [**High-Impact Projects:** Led projects with clear business impact, including a Redfin home value forecaster that boosted site visits 5–10% and a Netflix metadata automation tool that improved content discoverability.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1380.0)
  - [**Case Study Approach:** Proposed a classification model (e.g., XGBoost) to find content tactics that boost LLM visibility, prioritizing explainability to provide actionable insights.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3287.0)
  - [**Startup Fit:** Eager for a foundational role, having been the second data scientist at a prior startup (Arturo) and appreciating growthx.ai's technical leadership.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2443.0)

## Topics

### Candidate Background & Experience

  - [**Current Role (Integral Ad Science):**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=143.0)
      - [**Focus:** Content understanding for brand safety, using LLMs, vision, and audio models to detect content at scale.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=143.0)
      - [**Process:** Evaluate open-weight models → fine-tune/train → deploy → monitor performance.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=237.0)
      - [**Experimentation:** Uses A/B tests to compare model performance, tracking "flag rates" (false positives) as the primary production metric.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=665.0)
  - [**Key Projects:**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1123.0)
      - [**Netflix (Content Metadata Automation):**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1123.0)
          - [**Goal:** Automate tagging for 55k internal categories to expand coverage and improve human annotator efficiency.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1158.0)
          - [**Method:** Multimodal (text, audio, video) scene-level analysis using early LLMs and vision encoders.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1212.0)
          - [**Impact:** Increased content discoverability for low-tier titles in new markets.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1408.0)
      - [**Redfin (Home Value Forecasting):**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1574.0)
          - [**Goal:** Forecast home values 12+ months out for users and agents.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1574.0)
          - [**Method:** Time series ensemble combining macroeconomics with hierarchical models (e.g., XGBoost with lagged features).](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1870.0)
          - [**Impact:** Boosted site visits 5–10%; overwhelmingly positive agent NPS.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=1686.0)

### Case Study: Boosting LLM Search Visibility

  - [**Problem:** Identify content tactics (e.g., Q\&A, TLDRs) that increase brand visibility in LLM search results.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2566.0)
  - [**Proposed Solution:**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2692.0)
      - [**Data Collection:**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2692.0)
          - [Define "visibility" (appearance, competitor context, sentiment).](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2723.0)
          - [Use a fixed set of prompts to query LLM APIs (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini).](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2958.0)
          - [Scrape source webpages from results.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2958.0)
      - [**Feature Engineering:**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3040.0)
          - [Extract content features (e.g., presence of bullet points, quotes, visuals).](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3157.0)
          - [Analyze LLM-preferred sources (e.g., Reddit, licensed news).](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3044.0)
      - [**Modeling:**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3287.0)
          - [Frame as a classification task (brand appears vs. not).](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3287.0)
          - [**Model Choice:** XGBoost, as it handles feature interactions and provides explainability (feature importances) better than logistic regression.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3410.0)

### Role & Company Fit

  - [**growthx.ai Opportunity:**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2443.0)
      - [First data scientist hire; foundational role with broad responsibility.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2443.0)
      - [Initial focus is internal: build the data stack and optimize the content creation pipeline.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3517.0)
      - [Strong leadership support for a full ML stack (e.g., Snowflake, AWS, Docker, MLflow).](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3665.0)
  - [**Candidate Fit:**](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2443.0)
      - [Eager for a foundational role, having been the second data scientist at a prior startup (Arturo).](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=2487.0)
      - [Appreciates the technical leadership of Daniel (CEO), a key factor in taking the interview.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3786.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**growthx.ai:** Thakar to follow up with Reggie Edwards.](https://fathom.video/share/CLGS-Ai32pZqM9t9m826Pihzsk2o74Ms?tab=summary&timestamp=3835.0)
